_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: She has outlived every empire that ever tried to own her.

museum_objects.title: Croeseid Gold Coin of King Croesus
museum_objects.description: One of the earliest pure gold coins in history, struck under King Croesus of Lydia, legendary for his wealth.
museum_objects.significance: This is the coin that turned a rumor — 'rich as Croesus' — into something you could hold in your hand. Gold stopped being just beautiful and became the standard everything else would be measured against.
museum_objects.license: British Museum Standard Terms of Use
museum_objects.plain_language: One of the first-ever pure gold coins, made for a king so rich his name is still used today to describe someone wealthy.
museum_objects.licence: British Museum Standard Terms of Use

resolved.via: TIER 3 — no API, hard 403. Substitute with an open-licence equivalent.

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

museum_objects.title: Sardis Gold Croeseid
museum_objects.description: A gold Croeseid coin excavated at Sardis, the Lydian capital, where the coin type originated.
museum_objects.significance: Found in the actual capital where the gold standard was born — this coin never had to travel far to prove its point.
museum_objects.plain_language: A gold coin dug up in the exact city where gold coins were invented.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

display_alternatives.title: Gold stater. Lydia. ca. 560–546 B.C. The Metropolitan Museum of Art.jpg

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Gold Jewelry from the Great Death Pit, Ur
museum_objects.description: An elaborate gold earring recovered from the royal tombs of Ur in ancient Mesopotamia, predating coinage by two millennia.
museum_objects.significance: Long before anyone thought to stamp gold into coins, people already knew it was worth killing and dying for. This is that instinct in its rawest form.
museum_objects.plain_language: A gold earring buried with royalty over 4,500 years ago — proof that people have treasured gold since long before money existed.

image_qa.what_i_see: A collection of gold jewelry pieces from ancient Mesopotamia, including earrings, a necklace, and bracelets.
image_qa.why: The image shows a variety of gold jewelry items consistent with the description of 'Gold Jewelry from the Great Death Pit, Ur' and matches the context of ancient Mesopotamian royal tombs.

museum_objects.title: Gold Necklace from the Royal Cemetery at Ur
museum_objects.description: An elaborate gold necklace recovered by archaeologists from the royal tombs at Ur.
museum_objects.significance: Buried with kings and queens who believed they'd need it in the next life — that's how deep gold's hold on humanity runs.
museum_objects.license: British Museum Standard Terms of Use
museum_objects.plain_language: An ancient gold necklace buried with a king thousands of years before coins even existed.
museum_objects.licence: British Museum Standard Terms of Use

resolved.via: TIER 3 — no API, hard 403. Substitute with an open-licence equivalent.

museum_objects.title: Golden Funerary Mask of Agamemnon
museum_objects.description: A gold funerary mask discovered at Mycenae, one of the most famous gold artifacts of the Bronze Age Aegean.
museum_objects.significance: A face made of gold to survive death itself — the oldest idea about this metal there is: it doesn't decay, and it doesn't forget who wore it.
museum_objects.license: National Archaeological Museum of Athens terms
museum_objects.plain_language: A gold mask placed over a dead king's face over 3,500 years ago, because gold doesn't rot the way everything else does.
museum_objects.licence: National Archaeological Museum of Athens terms

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

world_map.map_description: Gold's story starts in the riverbeds of ancient Lydia and never really ends — it just keeps moving, from ancient temples to modern bank vaults.

nodes.label: Sardis, Lydia
nodes.description: Capital of the Lydian kingdom, where King Croesus minted the first pure gold coins.

nodes.label: Pactolus River
nodes.description: The gold-bearing river whose alluvial deposits made Lydia legendarily wealthy.

nodes.label: Temple of Artemis, Ephesus
nodes.description: One of the ancient world's great temples, funded partly by Lydian gold wealth and a pilgrimage site for centuries.

nodes.label: Bank of England Vaults
nodes.description: One of the largest modern gold repositories in the world, holding gold from nations across the globe.

nodes.label: Fort Knox, Kentucky
nodes.description: The famous U.S. gold depository, symbol of modern gold's role as a national reserve asset.

nodes.label: Las Médulas, Spain
nodes.description: Ancient Roman gold-mining site, showing how the pursuit of gold reshaped entire landscapes.
